Challenges to the definition of amateur
2010 and debate on removal from the Olympics
first_paragraph: [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") tournaments have been staged at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") since 1920. The men's tournament was introduced at the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") and was transferred permanently to the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") program in 1924. The women's tournament was first held at the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics").

[Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") tournaments have been staged at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") since 1920. The men's tournament was introduced at the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") and was transferred permanently to the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") program in 1924. The women's tournament was first held at the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics").

The Olympic Games were originally intended for [amateur athletes](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports"). However, the advent of the state-sponsored "full-time amateur athlete" of the Eastern Bloc countries further eroded the ideology of the pure amateur, as it put the self-financed amateurs of the Western countries at a disadvantage. The Soviet Union entered teams of athletes who were all nominally students, soldiers, or working in a profession, but many of whom were in reality paid by the state to train on a full-time basis.[[1]](#cite_note-Benjamin,_Daniel-1) In 1986, the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) voted to allow professional athletes to compete in the Olympic Games starting in 1988. The [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) was initially reluctant to allow its players to compete because the Olympics are held in the middle of the NHL season, and the league would have to halt play if many of its players participated. Eventually, NHL players were admitted starting in 1998.[[2]](#cite_note-schantz-2) The league skipped 2018 and 2022 editions but then agreed to return for 2026 and beyond.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From 1924 to 1988, the tournament started with a [round-robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") series of games and ended with the medal round. Medals were awarded based on points accumulated during that round. In 1992, the playoffs were introduced for the first time since [1920](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1920 Summer Olympics"). In 1998, the format of the tournament was adjusted to accommodate the NHL schedule; a preliminary round was played without NHL players or the top six teams—Canada, the Czech Republic, Finland, Russia, Sweden and the United States—followed by a final round which included them. The tournament format was changed again in 2006; every team played five preliminary games with the full use of NHL players.

The games of the tournament follow the [rules](/wiki/Ice_hockey_rules "Ice hockey rules") of the [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF), which differ slightly from the [rules used in the NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League_rules "National Hockey League rules"). In the men's tournament, [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the Winter Olympics") was the most successful team of the first three decades, winning six of seven gold medals from 1920 to 1952. [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_Olympics "Sweden at the Olympics") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Olympics "United States at the Olympics") were also competitive during this period and won multiple medals. Between 1920 and 1968, the Olympic hockey tournament was also counted as the [Ice Hockey World Championship](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships") for that year. The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_Olympics "Soviet Union at the Olympics") first participated in [1956](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1956 Winter Olympics") and overtook Canada as the dominant international team, winning seven of the nine tournaments in which they participated. The United States won gold medals in [1960](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1960 Winter Olympics") and [1980](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1980 Winter Olympics"), which included the "[Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")" upset of the Soviet Union. Canada went 50 years without a gold medal, before winning one in [2002](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics"), and following it with back-to-back wins in [2010](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2010 Winter Olympics") and [2014](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2014 Winter Olympics"). Other nations to win gold include [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics") in [1936](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1936 Winter Olympics"), the [Unified Team](/wiki/Unified_Team_at_the_Olympics "Unified Team at the Olympics") in [1992](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1992 Winter Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_Olympics "Sweden at the Olympics") in [1994](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [2006](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2006 Winter Olympics"), the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_Olympics "Czech Republic at the Olympics") in [1998](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_Olympics "Russia at the Olympics") (as [OAR](/wiki/Olympic_Athletes_from_Russia_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Olympic Athletes from Russia at the 2018 Winter Olympics")) in [2018](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2018 Winter Olympics"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_Olympics "Finland at the Olympics") in [2022](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2022 Winter Olympics") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Olympics "United States at the Olympics") in [2026](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2026_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2026 Winter Olympics"). Other medal-winning nations include [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_Olympics "Switzerland at the Olympics"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_Olympics "Germany at the Olympics") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_Olympics "Slovakia at the Olympics").

In July 1992, the IOC voted to approve women's hockey as an Olympic event; it was first held at the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") in Nagano. The Nagano Organizing Committee was hesitant to include the event because of the additional costs of staging the tournament, but an agreement was reached that limited the field to six teams, and ensured that no additional facilities would be built. The [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada women's national ice hockey team") teams have dominated the event. The [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States women's national ice hockey team") won the first tournament in 1998 and others in 2018 and 2026. Canada has won all of the other tournaments (2002–2014, 2022).

## Inception as an Olympic sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Inception as an Olympic sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Winnipegfalcons.jpg/330px-Winnipegfalcons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winnipegfalcons.jpg)

Representing Canada, the [Winnipeg Falcons](/wiki/Winnipeg_Falcons "Winnipeg Falcons") (*pictured en route to the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics")*) were the first Olympic champions in ice hockey.

The first Olympic ice hockey tournament took place at the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").[[4]](#cite_note-4) At the time, organized international ice hockey was still relatively new.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF), the [sport's governing body](/wiki/Sport_governing_body "Sport governing body"), was created on 15 May 1908, and was known as the *Ligue Internationale de Hockey sur Glace (LIHG)* until 1947.[[6]](#cite_note-6) At the 1914 [Olympic Congress](/wiki/Olympic_Congress "Olympic Congress") in Paris, ice hockey was added to the list of optional sports that Olympics organizers could include.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The decision to include ice hockey for the 1920 Summer Olympics was made in January, three months before the start of the Games.[[8]](#cite_note-Pod1920-8) Several occurrences led to the sport's inclusion in the programme. Five European nations had committed to participating in the tournament and the managers of Antwerp's Palais de Glace stadium refused to allow the building to be used for [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1920 Summer Olympics") unless ice hockey was included.[[8]](#cite_note-Pod1920-8) The IIHF considers the 1920 tournament to be the first [Ice Hockey World Championship](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships"). From then on, the two events occurred concurrently, and every Olympic tournament until [1968](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics") is counted as the World Championship.[[9]](#cite_note-Timeline-9) The Olympic Games were originally intended for [amateur athletes](/wiki/Amateur_sports "Amateur sports"), so the players of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) and other professional leagues were not allowed to play.[[10]](#cite_note-Num10-10)

The [first Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics") were held in 1924 in [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix"), France.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Chapter 1, article 6, of the 2007 edition of the [Olympic Charter](/wiki/Olympic_Charter "Olympic Charter") defines winter sports as "sports which are practised on snow or ice".[[12]](#cite_note-12) Ice hockey and [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_Olympic_Games "Figure skating at the Olympic Games") were permanently integrated in the Winter Olympics programme.[[13]](#cite_note-History-13) The IOC made the Winter Games a permanent fixture and they were held the same year as the Summer Games until 1992. Following that, further Winter Games have been held on the third year (i.e. [1994](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), [1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics"), etc.) of each Olympiad.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Men's tournament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Men's tournament")]

#### 1920 Summer Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1920 Summer Olympics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/1920_olympics_poster.jpg/250px-1920_olympics_poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:1920_olympics_poster.jpg)

The poster of the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics")

The [men's tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1920 Summer Olympics") held at the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") was organized by a committee that included future LIHG president [Paul Loicq](/wiki/Paul_Loicq "Paul Loicq"). The tournament used the [Bergvall System](/wiki/Bergvall_System "Bergvall System"), in which three rounds were played.[[15]](#cite_note-Num21-15) The first round was an elimination tournament that determined the gold medal winner. The second round consisted of the teams that were defeated by the gold medal winner; the winner of that round was awarded the silver medal. The final round was played between teams that had lost to the gold or silver medal winners; the winner of that round received the bronze medal.[[16]](#cite_note-Birth-16)

The tournament was played from 23 to 29 April and seven teams participated: [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Canada at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "United States at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Sweden at the 1920 Summer Olympics"), [France](/wiki/France_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "France at the 1920 Summer Olympics") and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_at_the_1920_Summer_Olympics "Belgium at the 1920 Summer Olympics"). Canada chose to send the [Allan Cup](/wiki/Allan_Cup "Allan Cup")-winning [Winnipeg Falcons](/wiki/Winnipeg_Falcons "Winnipeg Falcons"). The Swedish team consisted of mostly [bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy") players, many of whom had only started playing hockey in preparation for the tournament.[[16]](#cite_note-Birth-16) Canadian team manager [W. A. Hewitt](/wiki/W._A._Hewitt "W. A. Hewitt") refereed the first game played, an 8–0 win by Sweden versus
Belgium.[[17]](#cite_note-IIHF-honour-roll-17)

Canada won all three of the team's games in the first round and won the gold medal, defeating Sweden in the final and outscoring opponents 27–1.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In the two subsequent rounds, the United States and Czechoslovakia won the silver and bronze medals respectively.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The Bergvall System was criticized, especially in Sweden, because the Swedish team had to play six games (winning three) while the bronze medal-winning Czechoslovak team only had to play three (winning one). [Erik Bergvall](/wiki/Erik_Bergvall "Erik Bergvall"), the creator of the system, stated that it was used incorrectly and that a tournament of all of the losing teams from the first round should have been played for the silver medal.[[16]](#cite_note-Birth-16) Because of these criticisms, the Bergvall System was not used again for ice hockey.[[16]](#cite_note-Birth-16)

#### 1924–1936

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1924–1936")]

In [1924](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), the tournament was played in a [round-robin format](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament"), consisting of a preliminary round and a medal round. The medals were awarded based on win–loss records during the medal round.[[20]](#cite_note-20) This format was used until [1988](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1988 Winter Olympics"), although the number of teams and games played varied slightly. The [Toronto Granites](/wiki/Toronto_Granites "Toronto Granites"), representing [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), became one of the most dominant hockey teams in Olympic history, outscoring opponents 110–3, led by [Harry Watson](/wiki/Harry_Watson_(ice_hockey_b._1898) "Harry Watson (ice hockey b. 1898)"), who scored 36 goals.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1924 Winter Olympics") won silver and [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Great Britain at the 1924 Winter Olympics") won bronze.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Watson's 36 goals remains the tournament record for career goals. He also set the all-time record for career points with 36 (assists were not counted at the time), which stood until 2010.[[23]](#cite_note-Selanne-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-05472%2C_St._Moritz%2C_Winterolympiade.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-05472%2C_St._Moritz%2C_Winterolympiade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-05472,_St._Moritz,_Winterolympiade.jpg)

A game between Canada and Sweden during the [1928 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1928_Winter_Olympics "1928 Winter Olympics")

Eleven teams participated in the [1928 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1928_Winter_Olympics "1928 Winter Olympics") in [St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), Switzerland. The defending champion [Canadian team](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1928 Winter Olympics") was given a [bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") to the medal round and won all of its games by a combined score of 38–0.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The [Swedish](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1928 Winter Olympics") and [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1928 Winter Olympics") teams won their first medals—silver and bronze respectively—and a [German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1928 Winter Olympics") team participated for the first time, finishing ninth.[[25]](#cite_note-OR1928-25) At the [1932 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1932_Winter_Olympics "1932 Winter Olympics"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1932_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1932 Winter Olympics"), represented by the [Winnipeg Hockey Club](/wiki/Winnipeg_Hockey_Club "Winnipeg Hockey Club"), won gold in a tournament that consisted of four teams that played each other twice.[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_1932_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 1932 Winter Olympics") won bronze, the nation's first medal in the sport.[[27]](#cite_note-OR1932-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/1936_US_Olympic_Ice_Hockey_Team.jpg/250px-1936_US_Olympic_Ice_Hockey_Team.jpg)](/wiki/File:1936_US_Olympic_Ice_Hockey_Team.jpg)

The bronze medal-winning [1936 American Olympic team](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1936 Winter Olympics").

Two days before the [1936 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1936_Winter_Olympics "1936 Winter Olympics") in [Garmisch-Partenkirchen](/wiki/Garmisch-Partenkirchen "Garmisch-Partenkirchen"), Germany, Canadian officials protested that two players on the [British team](/wiki/Great_Britain_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Great Britain men's national ice hockey team")–[James Foster](/wiki/James_Foster_(ice_hockey) "James Foster (ice hockey)") and [Alex Archer](/wiki/Alex_Archer "Alex Archer")–had played in Canada but transferred without permission to play for clubs in the [English National League](/wiki/English_National_League "English National League"). The IIHF agreed with Canada, but Great Britain threatened to withdraw the team if the two were barred from competing. To avoid a conflict, Canada withdrew the protest shortly before the Games began. The tournament consisted of four groups and fifteen teams. [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "Great Britain at the 1936 Winter Olympics") became the first non-Canadian team to win gold; [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1936 Winter Olympics") won silver and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1936 Winter Olympics") bronze.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

#### Challenges to the definition of amateur

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Challenges to the definition of amateur")]

The [Canadian Amateur Hockey Association](/wiki/Canadian_Amateur_Hockey_Association "Canadian Amateur Hockey Association") (CAHA) revised its definition of amateur and broke away from the [Amateur Athletic Union of Canada](/wiki/Amateur_Athletic_Union_of_Canada "Amateur Athletic Union of Canada") in 1936, despite the possibility that its players may no longer be eligible for Olympic hockey.[[29]](#cite_note-Young-29) [Tommy Lockhart](/wiki/Tommy_Lockhart "Tommy Lockhart") founded the [Amateur Hockey Association of the United States](/wiki/USA_Hockey "USA Hockey") (AHAUS) in 1937, after disagreements with the [Amateur Athletic Union](/wiki/Amateur_Athletic_Union "Amateur Athletic Union") of the United States over international amateurs.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) The CAHA and the AHAUS joined to form the [International Ice Hockey Association](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Association "International Ice Hockey Association") in 1940.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Its president [W. G. Hardy](/wiki/W._G._Hardy "W. G. Hardy") sought for acceptance by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) on terms acceptable to the CAHA.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) CAHA president [George Dudley](/wiki/George_Dudley "George Dudley") subsequently threatened to withdraw Canada from the Olympics over the definition of amateur. An IOC decision on the matter was postponed when the [1940 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1940_Winter_Olympics "1940 Winter Olympics") and [1944 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1944_Winter_Olympics "1944 Winter Olympics") were cancelled due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[29]](#cite_note-Young-29)[[35]](#cite_note-35) In 1947, ice hockey's world governing body, the [LIHG](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation"), agreed to a merger with the International Ice Hockey Association, was subsequently renamed to the [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF), and recognized the AHAUS as the governing body of hockey in the United States instead of the AAU.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

#### 1948–1952

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1948–1952")]

The IIHF considered whether to have an ice hockey tournament at the [1948 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1948_Winter_Olympics "1948 Winter Olympics") in [St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), or host a separate [Ice Hockey World Championships](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "Ice Hockey World Championships") elsewhere in Switzerland in 1948.[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Avery Brundage](/wiki/Avery_Brundage "Avery Brundage") of the [United States Olympic Committee](/wiki/United_States_Olympic_%26_Paralympic_Committee "United States Olympic & Paralympic Committee") (USOC) reportedly campaigned to IIHF delegates to vote against inclusion of the AHAUS in the upcoming Olympics.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The LIHG passed a resolution that its teams would only play against teams approved by the CAHA and the AHAUS, which was accepted by the Swiss Olympic organizing committee.[[39]](#cite_note-nov-08-1947-39) Brundage threatened that the USOC would boycott the Olympics if the AHAUS team was recognized.[[39]](#cite_note-nov-08-1947-39) The Swiss Olympic organizing committee insisted on the AHAUS team being recognized, despite persistent charges by Brundage that the AHAUS team was "tainted with professionalism".[[40]](#cite_note-jan-27-1948-40) Brundage and the AAU supported a [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") team instead.[[40]](#cite_note-jan-27-1948-40) After bitter negotiations which were not resolved until the night before the Olympics, the AHAUS team was allowed to play in the tournament, but the IOC declared those games would not count in the standings.[[41]](#cite_note-feb-23-1960-41)

Both [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1948_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1948 Winter Olympics") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1948_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1948 Winter Olympics") won seven games and tied when they played each other. The gold medal winner was determined by [goal difference](/wiki/Goal_difference "Goal difference"): Canada won the gold because it had an average of 13.8 goals per game compared to Czechoslovakia's average of 4.3.[[42]](#cite_note-OR1948-42) Czechoslovakia's team was quickly improving; it won the [1947](/wiki/1947_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1947 World Ice Hockey Championships") and [1949 World Championships](/wiki/1949_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1949 World Ice Hockey Championships").[[43]](#cite_note-43) [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1948_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1948 Winter Olympics") won the bronze medal, and the AHAUS team finished fourth in the standings in 1948.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Pod1948-45)

Discussions began in 1950, whether or not ice hockey would be included in the [1952 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") hosted in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"). The IOC sought assurance that participating teams would adhere to its amateur code rather than what was accepted by the IIHF, and also wanted to exclude IIHF president [Fritz Kraatz](/wiki/Fritz_Kraatz "Fritz Kraatz") from negotiations. George Dudley and W. G. Hardy agreed there would be no negotiations on those terms, nor would they repudiate Kraatz. Dudley referred to the IOC as dictatorial and undemocratic, and expected the IIHF to discuss having its own 1952 Ice Hockey World Championships instead. He further stated that the Olympics would be a financial failure without the inclusion of hockey.[[46]](#cite_note-46) After discussions, hockey was ultimately included in the Olympics, and the gold medal was won by [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1952 Winter Olympics"), represented by the [Edmonton Mercurys](/wiki/Edmonton_Mercurys "Edmonton Mercurys"), for the second consecutive Games. It would be the last time that a Canadian team would win a gold medal in hockey for 50 years.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1952 Winter Olympics") won silver and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1952 Winter Olympics") won bronze. A team from [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") competed for the first time.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### 1956–1976

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1956–1976")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Vladislav_Tretiak.JPG/250px-Vladislav_Tretiak.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vladislav_Tretiak.JPG)

Russian goaltender [Vladislav Tretiak](/wiki/Vladislav_Tretiak "Vladislav Tretiak") of the Soviet Union is the only male athlete to have won three gold medals and one silver in ice hockey.

The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_ice_hockey_team "Soviet Union national ice hockey team") competed in its first World Championship in [1954](/wiki/1954_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1954 World Ice Hockey Championships"), defeating Canada and winning the gold medal.[[49]](#cite_note-Num25-49) At the [1956 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics") in [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo"), Italy, the [Soviet team](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1956 Winter Olympics") went undefeated and won its first gold medal. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1956 Winter Olympics"), represented by the [Kitchener-Waterloo Dutchmen](/wiki/Kitchener-Waterloo_Dutchmen_(ice_hockey) "Kitchener-Waterloo Dutchmen (ice hockey)"), lost to the Soviets and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1956 Winter Olympics") in the medal round, winning the bronze.[[49]](#cite_note-Num25-49) The [1960 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1960_Winter_Olympics "1960 Winter Olympics"), in [Squaw Valley](/wiki/Squaw_Valley,_Placer_County,_California "Squaw Valley, Placer County, California"), United States, saw the first, and to date only, team from [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Australia men's national ice hockey team") compete in the tournament. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1960 Winter Olympics"), the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1960 Winter Olympics"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1960 Winter Olympics") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1960 Winter Olympics") were the top four teams heading into the Games, but were all defeated by the [American team](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1960_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1960 Winter Olympics"), which won all seven games en route to its first Olympic gold medal. Canada won the silver medal and the Soviet Union won the bronze.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

At the [1964 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1964_Winter_Olympics "1964 Winter Olympics") in [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck"), Austria, the [Soviet team](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1964 Winter Olympics") won all seven of its games, earning the gold medal. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1964 Winter Olympics") finished the tournament with five wins and two losses, putting the team in a three-way tie for second place with [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1964 Winter Olympics") and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1964 Winter Olympics"). Before 1964, the tie-breaking procedure was based on goal difference in games against teams in the medal round; under that system, Canada would have placed third ahead of the Czechoslovak team. During the tournament the procedure was changed to take all games into consideration, which meant that the Canadians finished fourth.[[51]](#cite_note-51) At the time, the Olympics also served as the World Championships; under the IIHF's unchanged rules, Canada should have received bronze for the World Championships.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1968_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1968 Winter Olympics") won its third gold medal with a 7–1 record in the [1968 Grenoble Olympics](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics"). [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1968_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1968 Winter Olympics") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1968_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1968 Winter Olympics") won the silver and bronze medals, respectively.[[55]](#cite_note-OR1968-55) It was the last time that the Olympics were counted as the World Championships, as in [1972](/wiki/1972_Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "1972 Ice Hockey World Championships") the first standalone World Championship was held in an Olympic year. In 1970, Canada withdrew from international ice hockey competition protesting the use of full-time "amateurs" by the Soviet Union and Czechoslovakia,[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-Num17-58) and the team did not participate in the [1972](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics") and [1976 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1976_Winter_Olympics "1976 Winter Olympics").[[58]](#cite_note-Num17-58) Led by goaltender [Vladislav Tretiak](/wiki/Vladislav_Tretiak "Vladislav Tretiak") and forwards [Valeri Kharlamov](/wiki/Valeri_Kharlamov "Valeri Kharlamov"), [Alexander Yakushev](/wiki/Alexander_Yakushev "Alexander Yakushev"), [Vladimir Petrov](/wiki/Vladimir_Vladimirovich_Petrov "Vladimir Vladimirovich Petrov") and [Boris Mikhailov](/wiki/Boris_Mikhailov_(ice_hockey) "Boris Mikhailov (ice hockey)"), the Soviet team won gold at both the 1972 Games in [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"), Japan and 1976 Winter Olympics in Innsbruck, Austria.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In [1971](/wiki/1971_Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "1971 Ice Hockey World Championships"), the United States finished last at the World Championships and was relegated to [Pool B](/wiki/IIHF_World_Championship_Division_I#Group_B "IIHF World Championship Division I"). The team then qualified for the 1972 Olympics and won silver, making it the first Pool B team to win an Olympic medal.[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1972_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1972 Winter Olympics") won the bronze medal in 1972.[[61]](#cite_note-OR1972-61) In 1976, [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1976 Winter Olympics") won the silver and [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany_at_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "West Germany at the 1976 Winter Olympics") won bronze.[[62]](#cite_note-OR1976-62) Along with Canada, the Swedish team did not participate in the 1976 tournament, joining the Canadian boycott over the rules favoring the Soviet Union.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

#### 1980: "Miracle on Ice"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1980: \"Miracle on Ice\"")]

Main article: [Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/2002_Winter_Olympics_flame_-_Cropped.jpg/250px-2002_Winter_Olympics_flame_-_Cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:2002_Winter_Olympics_flame_-_Cropped.jpg)

The 1980 gold medal-winning American team had the honour of lighting the [Olympic cauldron](/wiki/Olympic_Flame "Olympic Flame") at the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The Winter Olympics returned to Lake Placid, New York in [1980](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics"). Twelve teams participated in the [tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1980 Winter Olympics"), including [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1980 Winter Olympics") for the first time since 1968. The Soviet Union had won the gold medal in five of the six previous Winter Olympic Games, and were the favorites to win once more in Lake Placid. The team consisted of full-time professional players with significant experience in international play. By contrast, the United States' team—led by head coach [Herb Brooks](/wiki/Herb_Brooks "Herb Brooks")—consisted exclusively of amateur players with mostly [college](/wiki/College_ice_hockey "College ice hockey") experience, and was the youngest team in the tournament and in U.S. national team history. In the group stage, both the Soviet and American teams were unbeaten; the U.S. achieved several notable results, including a 2–2 draw against Sweden, and a 7–3 upset victory over second-place favorites [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czechoslovakia men's national ice hockey team").

For the first game in the medal round, the United States played the Soviet Union. The first period finished tied at 2–2, and the Soviets led 3–2 following the second. The U.S. team then scored two more goals to take their first lead during the third and final period, winning the game 4–3. Following the game, the U.S. went on to clinch the gold medal by defeating [Finland](/wiki/Finland_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland men's national ice hockey team") in the final game. The Soviet Union took the silver medal by beating [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Sweden men's national ice hockey team").

The victory became one of the most iconic moments of the Winter Olympic Games and in U.S. sports. Equally well-known was the television call of the final seconds of the game by [Al Michaels](/wiki/Al_Michaels "Al Michaels") for [ABC](/wiki/Olympics_on_ABC "Olympics on ABC"), in which he said: "Do you believe in miracles?! YES!" In 1999, *[Sports Illustrated](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated "Sports Illustrated")* named the "[Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")" the top sports moment of the 20th century.[[65]](#cite_note-65) As part of its centennial celebration in 2008, the [International Ice Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Ice_Hockey_Federation "International Ice Hockey Federation") (IIHF) named the "Miracle on Ice" as the best international ice hockey story of the past 100 years.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### 1984–1994

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1984–1994")]

At the [1984 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1984_Winter_Olympics "1984 Winter Olympics") in [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo"), [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"), the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1984_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1984 Winter Olympics") won its sixth gold medal. [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1984_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1984 Winter Olympics") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1984_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1984 Winter Olympics") won the silver and bronze medals, respectively.[[67]](#cite_note-OR1984-67) The [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") were held in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), Canada, where the [Soviet team](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1988 Winter Olympics") captured its seventh and final gold medal. The Soviets' last Olympic game ended in a loss to [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1988 Winter Olympics"). The Finnish team was not considered a serious medal contender–it had competed in the World Championships since [1939](/wiki/1939_Ice_Hockey_World_Championships "1939 Ice Hockey World Championships") and had not won a single medal. However, Finland upset the Soviets 2–1 and won silver.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The IIHF decided to change the tournament format because in several cases, the gold medal winner had been decided before the final day of play. During a congress in 1990, the IIHF introduced a playoff system.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The new system was used at the [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") in [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville"), France. Preliminary round-robin games were held and followed by an eight-team cup-system style medal round that culminated in a gold medal game.[[9]](#cite_note-Timeline-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Helminen%27s_last_game.jpg/250px-Helminen%27s_last_game.jpg)](/wiki/File:Helminen%27s_last_game.jpg)

Finnish centre [Raimo Helminen](/wiki/Raimo_Helminen "Raimo Helminen"), a six-time Olympian, won a silver in 1988 and bronze medals in 1994 and 1998.[[70]](#cite_note-Num58-70)

Before 1989, players who lived in the Soviet Union, Czechoslovakia and other nations behind the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain") were not allowed to leave and play in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) by their respective governments.[[71]](#cite_note-Num65-71) Soviet officials agreed to allow some players to leave for the NHL following the [1989 World Championships](/wiki/1989_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "1989 World Ice Hockey Championships").[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) The Soviet Union dissolved in December 1991. Nine former Soviet states became part of the IIHF and started competing internationally, including the successor state [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team"), as well as [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Belarus men's national ice hockey team"), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Kazakhstan men's national ice hockey team"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Latvia men's national ice hockey team") and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_ice_hockey_team "Ukraine national ice hockey team").[[74]](#cite_note-74) At the 1992 Olympics, Russia and some other former Soviet states competed as one entity, known as the [Unified Team](/wiki/Unified_Team_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Unified Team at the 1992 Winter Olympics").[[75]](#cite_note-Num59-75) In the final, the Unified Team defeated [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1992 Winter Olympics") to win the gold medal while [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the 1992 Winter Olympics") won the bronze.[[75]](#cite_note-Num59-75)

Czechoslovakia split into the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Slovakia men's national ice hockey team") in January 1993. The IIHF recognized the Czech Republic as the successor to Czechoslovakia, allowing the team to retain its position in the top World Championship division, while Slovakia started in the lowest division ([Pool C](/wiki/IIHF_World_Championship_Division_II#Pool_C "IIHF World Championship Division II")) in 1994 and was forced to work its way up.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) Both nations competed in the tournament at the [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), as did [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1994 Winter Olympics"). [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1994 Winter Olympics") both finished the preliminary round undefeated, but Slovakia lost their medal round quarter-final game to Russia in [overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(ice_hockey) "Overtime (ice hockey)"). In the semi-finals, [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1994 Winter Olympics") defeated Russia and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1994 Winter Olympics") beat Finland, with the latter going on to win the bronze medal game. In the gold medal game between Sweden and Canada, a rematch of the group stage game that Canada won, both teams finished regulation and overtime play tied at 2–2. In the resulting [shootout](/wiki/Overtime_(ice_hockey)#Shootout "Overtime (ice hockey)"), the first in Olympic competition,[[80]](#cite_note-80) both nations scored two goals, which resulted in a sudden death shootout. [Peter Forsberg](/wiki/Peter_Forsberg "Peter Forsberg") of Sweden scored one of the most famous goals in Olympic history by faking a forehand shot, then sliding a one-handed backhand shot past goaltender [Corey Hirsch](/wiki/Corey_Hirsch "Corey Hirsch").[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Canada's final shooter [Paul Kariya](/wiki/Paul_Kariya "Paul Kariya")'s shot was saved by [Tommy Salo](/wiki/Tommy_Salo "Tommy Salo") and Sweden won the game and its first gold medal.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

#### 1998–2014

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1998–2014")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg/250px-Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nagano_1998-Russia_vs_Czech_Republic.jpg)

The 1998 gold medal game between [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team") and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") was the first played between teams consisting primarily of NHL players.

In 1995, an agreement to release NHL players to participate in the Olympics was reached between the IOC, IIHF, NHL, and [National Hockey League Players' Association](/wiki/National_Hockey_League_Players%27_Association "National Hockey League Players' Association") (NHLPA).[[84]](#cite_note-Gamble-84) The format of the [1998 tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament") was adjusted to accommodate the NHL's regular-season schedule. [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), considered a pre-tournament favourite, was upset in the semi-final round by the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and then lost the bronze medal game to [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1998 Winter Olympics").[[85]](#cite_note-85) Led by goaltender [Dominik Hašek](/wiki/Dominik_Ha%C5%A1ek "Dominik Hašek"), the Czech team defeated [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1998 Winter Olympics") in the final, winning its first gold medal in the sport.[[10]](#cite_note-Num10-10) Following the tournament, NHL commissioner [Gary Bettman](/wiki/Gary_Bettman "Gary Bettman") commented that it "was what we had predicted and hoped for from a pure hockey perspective, [it was] a wonderful tournament".[[86]](#cite_note-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/TeemuSelanne2010WinterOlympics.jpg/250px-TeemuSelanne2010WinterOlympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:TeemuSelanne2010WinterOlympics.jpg)

With 43 points, [Teemu Selänne](/wiki/Teemu_Sel%C3%A4nne "Teemu Selänne") of Finland is the all-time leading scorer in the men's tournament.[[23]](#cite_note-Selanne-23)

The next [tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament") was hosted in [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), United States. Finnish [centre](/wiki/Centre_(ice_hockey) "Centre (ice hockey)") [Raimo Helminen](/wiki/Raimo_Helminen "Raimo Helminen") became the first ice hockey player to compete in six tournaments.[[70]](#cite_note-Num58-70) In the quarter-finals, [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Belarus at the 2002 Winter Olympics") defeated [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 2002 Winter Olympics") in one of the biggest upsets since the Miracle on Ice.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) The team then lost to [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2002 Winter Olympics") in the semi-final and [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2002 Winter Olympics") in the bronze medal game, respectively.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The Canadian team rebounded from a disappointing first round and defeated the [American team](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 2006 Winter Olympics") in the gold medal game, winning their first gold medal in 50 years and seventh in men's hockey overall.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

The tournament format was again adjusted for [2006](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2006 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament"). In the semi-finals, [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 2006 Winter Olympics") defeated the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 2006 Winter Olympics"), and [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2006 Winter Olympics") defeated [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2006 Winter Olympics"). Sweden won the gold medal with a 3–2 victory over Finland and the Czech Republic won the bronze medal. Three months later, Sweden won the [2006 World Championships](/wiki/2006_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2006 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships") and became the first team to win the Olympic and World Championship gold in the same year.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Allegations have surfaced of Sweden throwing a game against [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 2006 Winter Olympics") so the Swedes would face [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 2006 Winter Olympics") in the quarter-finals instead of [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2006 Winter Olympics") or the Czech Republic. Shortly before the game, Sweden coach [Bengt-Åke Gustafsson](/wiki/Bengt-%C3%85ke_Gustafsson "Bengt-Åke Gustafsson") was reported to have publicly contemplated tanking in order to avoid those teams, saying about Canada and the Czechs, "One is cholera, the other the plague."[[92]](#cite_note-Farber-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Canada2010WinterOlympicsOTcelebration.jpg/250px-Canada2010WinterOlympicsOTcelebration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canada2010WinterOlympicsOTcelebration.jpg)

Members of the Canadian team celebrate moments after [Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby")'s gold medal-winning overtime goal at the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics").

The [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") were held in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada, the first time since NHL players started competing that the Olympics were held in a city with an NHL team. [Teemu Selänne](/wiki/Teemu_Sel%C3%A4nne "Teemu Selänne") of [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2010 Winter Olympics") scored his 37th point, breaking the record of 36 first set by Canadian Harry Watson in 1924 and later tied by [Vlastimil Bubník](/wiki/Vlastimil_Bubn%C3%ADk "Vlastimil Bubník") of Czechoslovakia and [Valeri Kharlamov](/wiki/Valeri_Kharlamov "Valeri Kharlamov") of the Soviet Union.[[23]](#cite_note-Selanne-23) [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 2010 Winter Olympics") made the final four for the first time, but lost the bronze medal game to Finland 3–5.[[93]](#cite_note-93) In the gold medal game, [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2010 Winter Olympics") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 2010 Winter Olympics") ended regulation play with a 2–2 tie, making it only the second Olympic gold medal match to go into overtime. Canadian player [Sidney Crosby](/wiki/Sidney_Crosby "Sidney Crosby") scored the winning goal 7:40 into overtime play to give Canada its eighth gold medal in men's hockey.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

The [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") were held in [Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), Russia, and retained the same game format used in Vancouver 2010, while returning to the larger IIHF-sized ice rinks.[[95]](#cite_note-95) [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Slovenia at the 2014 Winter Olympics") participated for the first time, upsetting [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 2014 Winter Olympics") in the round-robin before losing to [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 2014 Winter Olympics") in the quarterfinals 0–5, for its best finish in any international tournament.[[96]](#cite_note-96) [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Latvia at the 2014 Winter Olympics") upset [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 2014 Winter Olympics") 3–1 in the qualification playoffs, also making it to the Olympic quarterfinals for the first time, where they were narrowly defeated by [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2014 Winter Olympics") 2–1.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Host nation [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2014 Winter Olympics"), considered a pre-tournament favourite, lost 3–1 in the quarterfinals to [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2014 Winter Olympics") and finished fifth.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Entering the semi-finals undefeated after outscoring opponents 20–6, the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 2014 Winter Olympics") lost to Canada 0–1 and then lost the bronze medal game against [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2014 Winter Olympics") 0–5. Teemu Selänne scored six more points in the tournament, was named tournament MVP and boosted his modern-era Olympic career record for points to 43 (24 goals, 19 assists). At the age of 43, he also set records as both the oldest Olympic goal-scorer and oldest Olympic ice hockey medal winner.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Canada defeated Sweden 3–0 to win its ninth Olympic gold medal. The team did not trail at any point over the course of the tournament, and became the first back-to-back gold medal winner since the start of NHL participation in 1998.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

#### 2018–2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2018–2022")]

The [ice hockey tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament") at the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") in [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang "Pyeongchang") was held without participation of active NHL players for the first time since the 1994 Winter Olympics in Lillehammer.[[101]](#cite_note-latedeal-101) The favorite to win the gold medal was [Russia](/wiki/Russia_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia men's national ice hockey team") due to their domestic league, the [KHL](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League"), taking a customary Olympic break and allowing such stars as [Pavel Datsyuk](/wiki/Pavel_Datsyuk "Pavel Datsyuk") and [Ilya Kovalchuk](/wiki/Ilya_Kovalchuk "Ilya Kovalchuk") to play on the team.[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-103) As a consequence of a [doping scandal](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia"), the IOC suspended the [Russian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee "Russian Olympic Committee"), but allowed Russian athletes to compete under the Olympic flag after passing anti-doping tests.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The final was played between [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 2018 Winter Olympics"), who unexpectedly eliminated [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2018 Winter Olympics") in the semi-final,[[105]](#cite_note-105) and the [Olympic Athletes from Russia](/wiki/Olympic_Athletes_from_Russia_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Olympic Athletes from Russia at the 2018 Winter Olympics"). In the final, the Russian team prevailed, defeating Germany 4–3 in overtime and securing the gold medal after [Kirill Kaprizov](/wiki/Kirill_Kaprizov "Kirill Kaprizov") scored the winning goal. The Russian players sang the banned anthem during the medal ceremony, but the IOC decided not to pursue any action.[[106]](#cite_note-106) Canada won the bronze medal over the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 2018 Winter Olympics").[[107]](#cite_note-107)

Although NHL players were originally scheduled to participate at the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), the league and the NHL Players' Association announced on 21 December 2021 that they would be pulling out of the tournament, citing the impact of the ongoing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic")..[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) In the [tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2022 Winter Olympics") won their first-ever gold medal after going unbeaten and defeating the [Russian Olympic Committee](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee_athletes_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Russian Olympic Committee athletes at the 2022 Winter Olympics") 2–1 in the final. [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 2022 Winter Olympics") defeated [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 2022 Winter Olympics") 4–0 in the bronze medal game for their first-ever medal.[[111]](#cite_note-111) For the first time in history, the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 2022 Winter Olympics") did not qualify for the quarter-finals and finished in ninth place, their lowest placement in history.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Women's tournament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Women's tournament")]

#### Addition to the programme

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Addition to the programme")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Hayley_Wickenheiser_cropped.jpg/250px-Hayley_Wickenheiser_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hayley_Wickenheiser_cropped.jpg)

Canadian [Hayley Wickenheiser](/wiki/Hayley_Wickenheiser "Hayley Wickenheiser") is the all-time leading scorer in the women's tournament[[113]](#cite_note-113) and was named tournament MVP twice.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

At the [99th IOC Session](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings "List of IOC meetings") in July 1992, the IOC voted to approve women's hockey as an Olympic event beginning with the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") as part of their effort to increase the number of female athletes at the Olympics.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Women's ice hockey had not been in the programme when Nagano, Japan had won the right to host the Olympics in June 1991, and the decision required approval by the Nagano Winter Olympic Organizing Committee (NAOOC). The NAOOC was initially hesitant to include the event because of the additional costs of staging the tournament and because they felt their team, which had failed to qualify for [that year's World Championships](/wiki/1992_IIHF_Women%27s_World_Championship "1992 IIHF Women's World Championship"), could not be competitive.[[116]](#cite_note-Agreement-116) According to Glynis Peters, the [Canadian Amateur Hockey Association](/wiki/Canadian_Amateur_Hockey_Association "Canadian Amateur Hockey Association")'s (CAHA) head of female hockey, "the Japanese would have to finance an entirely new sports operation to bring their team up to Olympic standards in six years, which they were also really reluctant to do."[[117]](#cite_note-Approval-117) In November 1992, the NWOOC and IOC Coordination Committee reached an agreement to include a women's ice hockey tournament in the programme.[[116]](#cite_note-Agreement-116) Part of the agreement was that the tournament would be limited to six teams, and no additional facilities would be built. The CAHA also agreed to help build and train the Japanese team so that it could be more competitive.[[117]](#cite_note-Approval-117) The IOC had agreed that if the NAOOC had not approved the event, it would be held at the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics").[[116]](#cite_note-Agreement-116) The format of the first tournament was similar to the men's: preliminary round-robin games followed by a medal round playoff.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

#### 1998–2006

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1998–2006")]

Before 1998, women's hockey had been dominated by [Canada](/wiki/Canada_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada women's national ice hockey team"). Canadian teams had won every World Championship up to that point; however, by 1997, the [American team](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States women's national ice hockey team") had improved and was evenly matched with Canada. In thirteen games played between the two teams in 1997, Canada won seven and the United States won six. The 1998 Olympic tournament also included teams from [Finland](/wiki/Finland_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland women's national ice hockey team"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Sweden women's national ice hockey team"), [China](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "China women's national ice hockey team") and host [Japan](/wiki/Japan_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Japan women's national ice hockey team"). Canada and the United States dominated the round-robin portion. In their head-to-head match, the United States overcame a 4–1 deficit to win 7–4.[[119]](#cite_note-WHistory-119) The two teams met in the final, which the United States won 3–1 to become the third American ice hockey team to win Olympic gold. Finland defeated China 4–1 to win the bronze medal.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/SwedenUSAwomenshockey2002.jpg/250px-SwedenUSAwomenshockey2002.jpg)](/wiki/File:SwedenUSAwomenshockey2002.jpg)

A women's preliminary round game between [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 2002 Winter Olympics") and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 2002 Winter Olympics") in 2002

For the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), the number of teams was increased to eight with [Russia](/wiki/Russia_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia women's national ice hockey team"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Germany women's national ice hockey team") and [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Kazakhstan women's national ice hockey team") qualifying for the first time.[[121]](#cite_note-121) The Canadian and American teams went undefeated in the first round and semi-finals, setting up a gold medal rematch that the Canadian team won 3–2.[[122]](#cite_note-122) Following the game, members of the Canadian team accused the Americans of stomping on a Canadian flag in their dressing room, although an investigation later proved the rumour false.[[123]](#cite_note-123) The Swedish team won the bronze medal over Finland 2–1, the nation's first in women's ice hockey.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

In [2006](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2006 Winter Olympics"), Sweden defeated the US in a shootout in the semi-finals, marking the first time the US had lost to an opponent other than Canada.[[125]](#cite_note-125) The upset drew comparisons to the Miracle on Ice from 1980.[[126]](#cite_note-126) In the medal games, Canada defeated Sweden 4–1 to claim its second consecutive gold medal, while the Americans beat Finland 4–0 to win the bronze.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)

#### 2010 and debate on removal from the Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2010 and debate on removal from the Olympics")]

In [2010](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2010 Winter Olympics"), eight teams participated, including [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Slovakia women's national ice hockey team") for the first time.[[130]](#cite_note-130) In the gold medal game, Canada defeated the United States 2–0 to win their third consecutive gold. The [Finnish](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2010 Winter Olympics") team won the bronze medal over Sweden 3–2 OT, their first since 1998.[[131]](#cite_note-VSun2010-131)

The future of international women's ice hockey was discussed at the [World Hockey Summit](/wiki/World_Hockey_Summit "World Hockey Summit") in 2010, and dealt with how IIHF member associations could work together to grow the game and increase registration numbers, and the relative strength of the women's game in North America compared to the rest of the world.[[132]](#cite_note-132) [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") president [Jacques Rogge](/wiki/Jacques_Rogge "Jacques Rogge") raised concerns that the women's hockey tournament might be eliminated from the Olympics since the event was not competitively balanced and was dominated by Canada and the United States.[[133]](#cite_note-133) Team Canada captain [Hayley Wickenheiser](/wiki/Hayley_Wickenheiser "Hayley Wickenheiser") explained that the talent gap between the North American and European countries was due to the presence of women's professional leagues in North America, along with year-round training facilities. She stated the European players were talented, but their respective national team programs were not given the same level of support as the European men's national teams, or the North American women's national teams.[[134]](#cite_note-134) She stressed the need for women to have their own professional league which would be for the benefit of international hockey. IIHF vice-president [Murray Costello](/wiki/Murray_Costello "Murray Costello") promised to invest $2-million towards developing international women's hockey.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

#### 2014–2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2014–2022")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/%D0%A8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%86%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%96%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_2014.JPG/250px-%D0%A8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%86%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%96%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%A8%D0%B2%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%86%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%B8%D1%8F_%D0%96%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D1%81%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%8F_2014.JPG)

The [Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 2014 Winter Olympics") team celebrates shortly after winning the bronze medal at the 2014 Winter Olympics. It was the first medal for the country in women's hockey.

At the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics"), Canada defeated the United States 3–2, as [Marie-Philip Poulin](/wiki/Marie-Philip_Poulin "Marie-Philip Poulin") scored at 8:10 of overtime to win their fourth consecutive gold, rebounding from a two-nothing deficit late in the game. With the win, Canadians [Hayley Wickenheiser](/wiki/Hayley_Wickenheiser "Hayley Wickenheiser"), [Jayna Hefford](/wiki/Jayna_Hefford "Jayna Hefford") and [Caroline Ouellette](/wiki/Caroline_Ouellette "Caroline Ouellette") became the first athletes to win four ice hockey gold medals. They also joined Soviet biathlete [Alexander Tikhonov](/wiki/Alexander_Tikhonov "Alexander Tikhonov") and German speedskater [Claudia Pechstein](/wiki/Claudia_Pechstein "Claudia Pechstein") as the only athletes to win gold medals in four straight Winter Olympics.[[136]](#cite_note-136) In the bronze medal game [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 2014 Winter Olympics") beat Sweden 4–3 to win their first women's medal.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

In [2018](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States_national_women%27s_ice_hockey_team "United States national women's ice hockey team") defeated [Canada](/wiki/Canada_national_women%27s_ice_hockey_team "Canada national women's ice hockey team") for the gold medal in a shootout, winning 3–2. The Americans' winning the gold medal game marks the first time in 20 years that the United States took home a gold medal in women's hockey. They previously won in [1998](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament") in [Nagano, Japan](/wiki/Nagano,_Nagano "Nagano, Nagano"), which was also against Canada.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Canada's loss effectively ended their winning streak of four consecutive winter games, having won since [2002](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament").[[139]](#cite_note-139)

The [2022 edition](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament") was played with ten teams for the first time.[[140]](#cite_note-140) [Canada](/wiki/Canada_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Canada women's national ice hockey team") won their fifth gold medal, defeating the United States in the final 3–2.[[141]](#cite_note-141) [Finland](/wiki/Finland_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Finland women's national ice hockey team") defeated [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Switzerland women's national ice hockey team") 4–0 for the bronze medal.[[142]](#cite_note-142) The final standings were a repeat of the [2021 IIHF Women's World Championship](/wiki/2021_IIHF_Women%27s_World_Championship "2021 IIHF Women's World Championship").[[143]](#cite_note-143)

## Rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Rules")]

### Qualification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Qualification")]

Since 1976, 12 teams have participated in the men's tournament, except in 1998 and 2002, when the number was raised to 14. The number of teams has ranged from 4 (in 1932) to 16 (in 1964). After the NHL allowed its players to compete at the 1998 Winter Olympics, the "[Big Six](/wiki/Big_Six_(hockey) "Big Six (hockey)")" teams (Canada, the Czech Republic, Finland, Russia, Sweden and the United States) were given automatic qualification and [byes](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") to the final round.[[144]](#cite_note-Num7-144) The number of teams was increased to 14 so that a preliminary round-robin tournament consisting of eight teams could be held. The top two teams from the preliminary round ([Belarus](/wiki/Belarus_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Belarus at the 1998 Winter Olympics") and [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Kazakhstan at the 1998 Winter Olympics")) joined the "Big Six" in the finals. A similar system was used in 2002.[[145]](#cite_note-Bench-145) For the following tournament, the number of teams was lowered to 12 so that all teams played fewer games.[[146]](#cite_note-SR06-146) Qualification for the men's tournament at the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") was structured around the [2008 IIHF World Ranking](/wiki/2008_IIHF_World_Ranking "2008 IIHF World Ranking"). Twelve spots were made available for teams. The top nine teams in the World Ranking after the [2008 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships](/wiki/2008_Men%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2008 Men's World Ice Hockey Championships") received automatic berths. Teams ranked 19th through 30th played in a first qualification round in November 2008. The top three teams from the round advanced to the second qualification round, joined by teams ranked 10th through 18th. The top three teams from this round advanced to the Olympic tournament.[[147]](#cite_note-MenProg-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)

The women's tournament uses a similar qualification format. The top six teams in the IIHF Women's World Ranking after the [2008 Women's World Ice Hockey Championships](/wiki/2008_Women%27s_World_Ice_Hockey_Championships "2008 Women's World Ice Hockey Championships") received automatic berths. Teams ranked 13th and below were divided into two groups for a first qualification round in September 2008. The two group winners advanced to the second qualification round, where the teams ranked seventh through twelfth joined them.[[149]](#cite_note-WomProg-149)

### Players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Players")]

#### Eligibility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Eligibility")]

The IIHF lists the following requirements for a player to be eligible to play in international tournaments:[[150]](#cite_note-Eligibility-150)

* "Each player must be under the jurisdiction of an IIHF member national association."
* "Each player must be a citizen of the country he/she represents."

If a player who has never played in an IIHF competition changes their citizenship, they must participate in national competitions in their new country for at least two consecutive years and have an international transfer card (ITC).[[150]](#cite_note-Eligibility-150) If a player who has previously played in an IIHF tournament wishes to change their national team, they must have played in their new country for four years. A player can only do this once.[[150]](#cite_note-Eligibility-150) The original IOC rules stated that an athlete that had already played for one nation could not later change nations under any circumstances.[[8]](#cite_note-Pod1920-8)

#### Use of professional players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Use of professional players")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Baron_Pierre_de_Coubertin.jpg/250px-Baron_Pierre_de_Coubertin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baron_Pierre_de_Coubertin.jpg)

[Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin"), founder of the IOC

[Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin"), founder of the IOC, was influenced by the ethos of the aristocracy as exemplified in the [English public schools](/wiki/Independent_school_(UK) "Independent school (UK)").[[151]](#cite_note-OIdeology-151) The public schools subscribed to the belief that sport formed an important part of education and there was a prevailing concept of fairness in which practicing or training was considered cheating.[[151]](#cite_note-OIdeology-151) As class structure evolved through the 20th century, the definition of the amateur athlete as an aristocratic gentleman became outdated.[[151]](#cite_note-OIdeology-151) The advent of the state-sponsored "full-time amateur athlete" of the Eastern Bloc countries further eroded the ideology of the pure amateur, as it put the self-financed amateurs of the Western countries at a disadvantage. The Soviet Union entered teams of athletes who were all nominally students, soldiers, or working in a profession, but many of whom were in reality paid by the state to train on a full-time basis.[[1]](#cite_note-Benjamin,_Daniel-1) Nevertheless, the IOC held to the traditional rules regarding amateurism until 1988.[[2]](#cite_note-schantz-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/1998_Winter_Olympics_medals.JPG/250px-1998_Winter_Olympics_medals.JPG)](/wiki/File:1998_Winter_Olympics_medals.JPG)

A set of silver, gold and bronze medals from the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") on display at the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame")

Near the end of the 1960s, the [Canadian Amateur Hockey Association](/wiki/Canadian_Amateur_Hockey_Association "Canadian Amateur Hockey Association") (CAHA) felt their amateur players could no longer be competitive against the Soviet team's full-time athletes and the other constantly improving European teams. They pushed for the ability to use players from professional leagues but met opposition from the IIHF and IOC. At the IIHF Congress in 1969, the IIHF decided to allow Canada to use nine non-NHL professional hockey players[[58]](#cite_note-Num17-58) at the 1970 World Championships in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), Canada.[[152]](#cite_note-152) The decision was reversed in January 1970 after IOC President [Brundage](/wiki/Avery_Brundage "Avery Brundage") said that ice hockey's status as an Olympic sport would be in jeopardy if the change was made.[[58]](#cite_note-Num17-58) In response, Canada withdrew from international ice hockey competition and officials stated that they would not return until "open competition" was instituted.[[58]](#cite_note-Num17-58)[[153]](#cite_note-153) [Günther Sabetzki](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther_Sabetzki "Günther Sabetzki") became president of the IIHF in 1975 and helped to resolve the dispute with the CAHA. In 1976, the IIHF agreed to allow "open competition" between all players in the World Championships. However, NHL players were still not allowed to play in the Olympics, because of the unwillingness of the NHL to take a break mid-season and the IOC's amateur-only policy.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

Before the 1984 Winter Olympics, a dispute formed over what made a player a professional. The IOC had adopted a rule that made any player who had signed an NHL contract but played less than ten games in the league eligible. However, the United States Olympic Committee maintained that any player contracted with an NHL team was a professional and therefore not eligible to play. The IOC held an emergency meeting that ruled NHL-contracted players were eligible, as long as they had not played in any NHL games.[[155]](#cite_note-Pod1984-155) This made five players on Olympic rosters—one Austrian, two Italians and two Canadians—ineligible. Players who had played in other professional leagues—such as the [World Hockey Association](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association")—were allowed to play.[[155]](#cite_note-Pod1984-155) Canadian hockey official [Alan Eagleson](/wiki/Alan_Eagleson "Alan Eagleson") stated that the rule was only applied to the NHL and that professionally contracted players in European leagues were still considered amateurs.[[156]](#cite_note-156) [Murray Costello](/wiki/Murray_Costello "Murray Costello") of the CAHA suggested that a Canadian withdrawal was possible.[[157]](#cite_note-157) In 1986, the IOC voted to allow all athletes to compete in the Olympic Games starting in 1988.[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)

#### NHL participation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: NHL participation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Finlande_Russie.jpg/250px-Finlande_Russie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Finlande_Russie.jpg)

NHL players [Saku Koivu](/wiki/Saku_Koivu "Saku Koivu") of Finland and [Pavel Datsyuk](/wiki/Pavel_Datsyuk "Pavel Datsyuk") of Russia face off in a semi-final game at the 2006 Winter Olympics.

The NHL decided not to allow all players to participate in 1988, 1992, 1994, 2018, and 2022 because the Winter Olympics typically occur in February, during the league's regular season. To allow participation, the NHL would have been forced to take a break in its schedule.[[160]](#cite_note-160)

In 1992, [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) players participated in the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics").
