NHL commissioner [Gary Bettman](/wiki/Gary_Bettman "Gary Bettman") (an NBA executive in 1992) commented that the "[NBA]'s worldwide awareness grew dramatically". He hoped that NHL participation would "get exposure like the world has never seen for hockey".[[84]](#cite_note-Gamble-84) The typical NBA season is held in the winter and spring, so the Summer Olympics do not conflict with the regular season schedule. Bettman "floated a concept of moving hockey to the Summer Games", but this was rejected because of the Olympic Charter.[[84]](#cite_note-Gamble-84) In March 1995, Bettman, René Fasel, IOC president [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch") and NHLPA executive director [Bob Goodenow](/wiki/Bob_Goodenow "Bob Goodenow") met in Geneva, Switzerland. They reached an agreement that allowed NHL players to participate in the Olympics, starting with the 1998 Games in [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano,_Nagano "Nagano, Nagano"), Japan.[[84]](#cite_note-Gamble-84) The deal was officially announced by the NHL on 2 October 1995. Bettman said: "We're doing this to build the game of hockey, pure and simple, we think whatever benefits are recouped, it will end up making this game bigger, stronger and healthier."[[161]](#cite_note-161)

The [2004–05 NHL season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_season "2004–05 NHL season") was [locked out](/wiki/Lockout_(industry) "Lockout (industry)") and eventually [cancelled](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_NHL_lockout "2004–05 NHL lockout") because of a labour dispute between the league and its players. In January 2005, Bettman commented that he was hesitant to allow league participation in the Olympics because he did not like the idea of stopping play mid-season after the cancellation of the previous season.[[162]](#cite_note-162) The lockout was resolved in July 2005 and the newly negotiated [NHL Collective Bargaining Agreement](/wiki/NHL_Collective_Bargaining_Agreement "NHL Collective Bargaining Agreement") allowed league participation in the [2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") and [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Some NHL team owners were against their players participating in the tournament because of concerns about injury or exhaustion. [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") owner [Ed Snider](/wiki/Ed_Snider "Ed Snider") commented that "I'm a believer in the Olympics and I think it's good for the NHL to participate, having said that, the people who participate should be the ones who are absolutely healthy."[[163]](#cite_note-163) Some NHL players used the break as an opportunity to rest and did not participate in the tournament,[[164]](#cite_note-164) and several players were injured during the Olympics and were forced to miss NHL games. Bettman said that several format changes were being discussed so that the tournament would be "a little easier for everybody".[[165]](#cite_note-165)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/AlexanderOvechkin2010WinterOlympicslineup.jpg/250px-AlexanderOvechkin2010WinterOlympicslineup.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlexanderOvechkin2010WinterOlympicslineup.jpg)

Prior to the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") in Russia, a number of Russian NHL players, including [Alexander Ovechkin](/wiki/Alexander_Ovechkin "Alexander Ovechkin"), stated that they would play in the tournament with or without the NHL's approval.[[166]](#cite_note-Ovechkin-166)

It was originally thought that for NHL participating in the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") a deal would have to be negotiated between the NHL and NHLPA in the [Collective Bargaining Agreement](/wiki/NHL_Collective_Bargaining_Agreement "NHL Collective Bargaining Agreement").[[167]](#cite_note-Kelly-167) In January 2013, the NHL and NHLPA agreed on a new Collective Bargaining Agreement.[[168]](#cite_note-168) However, the decision on NHL participation at the Olympics was later announced on 19 July 2013. As part of the deal, the NHL will go on break for 17 days during the Olympics and will send 13 on-ice officials to help with the Games.[[169]](#cite_note-169) NHL management was hesitant to commit to the tournament; Bettman argued the Olympic break is a "strain on the players, on the schedule and on fans", adding that "the benefits we get tend to be greater when the Olympics are in North America than when they're in distant time zones."[[170]](#cite_note-170) According to Bettman, most of the NHL team owners agree with his position, and feel that the league does not receive enough benefits to justify the schedule break and risk of player injuries.[[171]](#cite_note-LeBrun-171) IIHF president [René Fasel](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Fasel "René Fasel") expressed his willingness to ensure NHL participation and vowed that he would "work day and night to have [NHL players](/wiki/NHL_players "NHL players") in Sochi".[[172]](#cite_note-172)

At an October 2008 press conference, then-NHLPA executive director [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(lawyer) "Paul Kelly (lawyer)") stated that the players want to return to the Olympics and would try to include the ability in the next agreement.[[171]](#cite_note-LeBrun-171) Russian NHL players [Alexander Ovechkin](/wiki/Alexander_Ovechkin "Alexander Ovechkin") and [Evgeni Malkin](/wiki/Evgeni_Malkin "Evgeni Malkin") stated that they want to participate in the tournament and would do so without the permission of the NHL, if necessary.[[166]](#cite_note-Ovechkin-166) Paul Kelly also believed that the NHL's strained relationship with the [Ice Hockey Federation of Russia](/wiki/Ice_Hockey_Federation_of_Russia "Ice Hockey Federation of Russia") and the [Kontinental Hockey League](/wiki/Kontinental_Hockey_League "Kontinental Hockey League") (KHL) could affect participation.[[167]](#cite_note-Kelly-167) In a 2009 interview, KHL president [Alexander Medvedev](/wiki/Alexander_Medvedev "Alexander Medvedev") claimed that the unwillingness of NHL officials to immediately commit to the Sochi Games was "an instrument of pressure" to force a transfer agreement between the two leagues.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

A major sticking point over NHL participation has been the insurance of players; for the 2014 Winter Olympics, the IOC paid around US$7 million to insure NHL players participating in the Games. In April 2016, the IOC announced that beginning in [2018](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics"), it would no longer cover accommodations, insurance, or travel for NHL players in the Olympics, prompting the IIHF to ask for support from national ice hockey associations and National Olympic Committees to help cover costs; Matti Nurminen of the Finnish Ice Hockey Association argued that it was the responsibility of the event's organizer to cover costs, and that "In our opinion, the same party should pay the bills, and that's not us. All the countries replied to the IIHF that they are not willing to pay for the insurance or the travel or any of the other expenses that are related to having the NHL players participate in Pyeongchang." *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* felt that the removal of this financial support would put NHL participation at Pyeongchang in jeopardy, noting the already-strenuous relationship between the NHL and the IOC; Gary Bettman noted that the NHL does not profit from their presence, adding that "in fact, we kind of disappear for two weeks because historically the IOC hasn't even let us join in promoting our participation in the Olympics."[[174]](#cite_note-NYTimes20160520-174)

On 3 April 2017, the NHL announced that it would not participate in the 2018 Winter Olympics. This decision was made primarily due to financial disagreements with the IOC over who would cover the costs of travel, insurance and accommodation. The agreement prior to the 1998 Winter Olympics in Nagano had the IOC covering these costs, but the IOC refused to extend that agreement ahead of the 2018 Games.[[175]](#cite_note-175) In their statement, the NHL said that it had been open to hearing from the IOC, the IIHF, and the players' association on ways to make Olympic participation more attractive to team owners, but no meaningful dialogue on that matter had materialized. As to reasons the Board of Governors might be interested in re-evaluating their strongly held views on the subject, the NHLPA "confirmed that it has no interest or intention of engaging in any discussion that might make Olympic participation more attractive to the Clubs", and that it would not schedule a break for the Olympics in the 2017–18 season.[[176]](#cite_note-sn-nonhl2018-176)

Although in the following months the president of the IIHF René Fasel tried to convince NHL to change its decision,[[177]](#cite_note-177) in September he stated that there was no chance for participation of NHL players in the Pyeongchang olympic tournament. "I can say that this is now gone. We can tick that off the list. We will have to look ahead to China and the Beijing 2022 Winter Games because there is an interest of the league and we have noted that. But logistically it is practically impossible for Pyeongchang. That train has left the station", he said.[[101]](#cite_note-latedeal-101) Some NHL players expressed their discontent with league's decision to skip the Olympics.[[178]](#cite_note-178) Alexander Ovechkin, captain of the Washington Capitals: "The Olympics are in my blood and everybody knows how much I love my country",[[179]](#cite_note-179) adding that "he would compete with Russia if he were the only NHL player to travel to South Korea".[[101]](#cite_note-latedeal-101) "It's brutal, [...] I don't think there's any reason we shouldn't be going", said Justin Faulk, 2014 Olympian and alternate captain for the Carolina Hurricanes.[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) After skipping the 2018 Olympics, the league intended to return for the 2022 Games in Beijing but pulled out at the last minute over the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[182]](#cite_note-182)[[183]](#cite_note-183) The league then officially returned for the 2026 and 2030 Games.[[184]](#cite_note-184)

### Game rules

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Game rules")]

At the first tournament in 1920, there were many differences from the modern game: games were played outdoors on natural ice, forward passes were not allowed,[[16]](#cite_note-Birth-16) the rink (which had been intended to be used only for figure skating) was 56 m × 18 m (165 ft × 58.5 ft)[[8]](#cite_note-Pod1920-8) and two 20-minute periods were played.[[15]](#cite_note-Num21-15) Each team had seven players on the ice, the extra position being the [rover](/wiki/Rover_(ice_hockey) "Rover (ice hockey)").[[9]](#cite_note-Timeline-9) Following the tournament, the IIHF held a congress and decided to adopt the Canadian rules—six men per side and three periods of play.[[16]](#cite_note-Birth-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Ice_hockey_layout.svg/330px-Ice_hockey_layout.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ice_hockey_layout.svg)

In 2010, games were played on an NHL-sized rink surface for the first time (*size comparison pictured*).

The tournaments follow the rules used by the IIHF. At the 1969 IIHF Congress, officials voted to allow [body-checking](/wiki/Checking_(ice_hockey) "Checking (ice hockey)") in all three zones in a [rink](/wiki/Ice_hockey_rink "Ice hockey rink") similar to the NHL; it's prohibited for women. Before that, body-checking was only allowed in the [defending zone](/wiki/Ice_hockey_rink#Zones "Ice hockey rink") in international hockey[[185]](#cite_note-Num19-185) Several other rule changes were implemented in the early 1970s: players were required to wear [helmets](/wiki/Hockey_helmet "Hockey helmet") starting in 1970, and [goaltender masks](/wiki/Goaltender_mask "Goaltender mask") became mandatory in 1972.[[9]](#cite_note-Timeline-9) In 1992, the IIHF switched to using a playoff system to determine medalists and decided that tie games in the medal round would be decided in a [shootout](/wiki/Penalty_shootout "Penalty shootout").[[186]](#cite_note-186) In 1998, the IIHF passed a rule that allowed two-line [passes](/wiki/Pass_(ice_hockey) "Pass (ice hockey)"). Before then, the [neutral zone trap](/wiki/Neutral_zone_trap "Neutral zone trap") had slowed the game down and reduced scoring.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

The current IIHF rules differ slightly from the [rules used in the NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League_rules "National Hockey League rules").[[188]](#cite_note-188) One difference between NHL and IIHF rules is standard rink dimensions: the NHL rink is narrower, measuring 61 m × 26 m (200 ft × 85 ft), instead of the international size of 61 m × 30.5 m (200 ft × 100 ft)[[189]](#cite_note-189) The larger international size allows for a faster and less physical style of play.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191) Another rule difference between the NHL and the IIHF rules concerns how [icing](/wiki/Icing_(ice_hockey) "Icing (ice hockey)") is called. In the NHL, a [linesman](/wiki/Official_(ice_hockey)#Linesmen "Official (ice hockey)") stops play due to icing if a defending player (other than the goaltender) is not behind an attacking player in the race to the end-zone faceoff dots in his defensive zone,[[192]](#cite_note-icing-192) in contrast to the IIHF rules in which play is stopped the moment the puck crosses the goal line.[[192]](#cite_note-icing-192) The NHL and IIHF also differ in penalty rules. The NHL calls five-minute major penalties for more dangerous infractions of the rules, such as fighting, in addition to the [minor and double minor penalties](/wiki/Penalty_(ice_hockey)#Types_of_penalties "Penalty (ice hockey)") called in IIHF games.[[193]](#cite_note-major-193) This is in contrast to the IIHF rule, by which players who fight risk a game misconduct & major penalties.[[194]](#cite_note-194) Beginning with the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_NHL_season "2005–06 NHL season"), the NHL instituted several new rules. Some were already used by the IIHF, such as the shootout and the two-line pass.[[195]](#cite_note-195) Others were not picked up by the IIHF, such as those requiring smaller [goaltender](/wiki/Goaltender "Goaltender") equipment and the addition of the [goaltender trapezoid](/wiki/Ice_hockey_rink#Goaltender_trapezoid "Ice hockey rink") to the rink.[[196]](#cite_note-196) However, the IIHF did agree to follow the NHL's zero-tolerance policy on obstruction and required referees to call more [hooking](/wiki/Hooking_(ice_hockey) "Hooking (ice hockey)"), holding, and interference penalties.[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198)

Each team is allowed to have between 15 and 20 skaters ([forwards](/wiki/Forward_(ice_hockey) "Forward (ice hockey)") and defencemen) and two or three goaltenders, all of whom must be citizens of the nation they play for.[[199]](#cite_note-MTP-199)[[200]](#cite_note-200)

### Banned substances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ice_hockey_at_the_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Banned substances")]

Main article: [Doping at the Olympic Games](/wiki/Doping_at_the_Olympic_Games "Doping at the Olympic Games")

The IIHF follows the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency")'s (WADA) regulations on [performance-enhancing drugs](/wiki/Use_of_performance-enhancing_drugs_in_sport "Use of performance-enhancing drugs in sport"). The IIHF maintains a Registered Testing Pool, a list of top players who are subjected to random in-competition and out-of-competition drug tests.[[201]](#cite_note-201) According to the WADA, a positive in-competition test results in disqualification of the player and a suspension that varies based on the number of offences. When a player tests positive, the rest of their team is subjected to testing; another positive test can result in a disqualification of the entire team.[[202]](#cite_note-202)[[203]](#cite_note-203)[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206)

In late 2005, two NHL players who had been listed as potential Olympians failed drug tests administered by the WADA. [Bryan Berard](/wiki/Bryan_Berard "Bryan Berard") tested positive for [19-Norandrosterone](/wiki/19-Norandrosterone "19-Norandrosterone").[[222]](#cite_note-222) [José Théodore](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Th%C3%A9odore "José Théodore") failed a drug test because he was taking [Propecia](/wiki/Finasteride "Finasteride"), a hair loss medication that contains the non-performance-enhancing drug Finasteride.[[223]](#cite_note-223)[[224]](#cite_note-224) Both players received two-year bans from international competition, although neither had made their team's final roster.[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[226]](#cite_note-226) On 6 December 2017 six Russian women ice hockey players were disqualified for doping violations. Results of the [Russian women's team](/wiki/Russia_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Russia women's national ice hockey team") at the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 2014 Winter Olympics – Women's tournament") were made void.[[227]](#cite_note-227) Two other Russian players, [Tatiana Burina](/wiki/Tatiana_Burina "Tatiana Burina") and [Anna Shukina](/wiki/Anna_Shukina "Anna Shukina"), were also disqualified ten days later.[[228]](#cite_note-International_Olympic_Committee-228)
